textPlain: I recently researched USB connected information displays but I am interested in e-Ink. I want- USB power + data- Open interface so I can drive it from my own software on the host (but not like a traditional monitor, I imagine more uploading pre-rendered bitmaps)- Image retention when powered off- High resolution paper like appearance- Between A5 and A4 in size- At least black, red and yellow as colors- Buttons or a way to connect buttons would be a bonusIf anyone has a tip, I'd be grateful.
Aand it's gone https://www.aliexpress.com/item/1005009941797169.html

For some reason, nothing says "future" to me more than having tiny screens embedded where they're not absolutely needed.When I grew up in the 90s and 00s, screens were definitely the most expensive part of any system they belonged to. And any gadget that came with its own screen attached to it was regarded as a delicacy only for the elite.Living long enough to see "disposable" screens cheaper than literal candy getting attached everywhere makes me happy.Can't wait to see Gemini-2.5 Pro-level LLMs embedded inside single post-it notes and thrown away like it's no big deal.
Seems perfect for a YubiKey type of device. Know where your authenticating to.
I feel like the winning move here would be to put the screen on a ball joint or hinge to give more options than just “face forward” v. “face backward”.
One thing I would like is a small portable hdmi display to use with my headless servers when they fail to boot. Even better would show screen over network.
What was that extra bar of information called on Macbooks?Were they a success?
Would be cool if I could reprogram it for some other output, because I don’t really see the need for this except for maybe on a raspberry pi “server”.
Curious this is so highly voted. These displays are absolutely tiny.
I really want a little USB drive with an eink display on it so I can set a label for each of my drives
Neat! This is exactly what I was looking for the other day.

Alarm bells always go off for me when a vendor, as here, blatantly Photoshops an idealized perfectly black, flat, and non-reflective mockup image of what would be displayed onto the picture of the real display.
AAAAANnndd its out of stock.  Nice work HN!
dead link

>>Between A5 and A4 in sizeVery expensive.For $59 you can get M5PaperS3 ESP32S3 Development Kit (960x540, 4.7" eInk Display, 235 ppi)https://shop.m5stack.com/products/m5papers3-esp32s3-developm...Or you can get:https://lilygo.cc/products/t5-e-paper-s3-proBut these have 4.7 inch display.
Seeed Studio is pumping out somewhat smaller sized e-ink with relatively open hardware for Trminal usehttps://www.cnx-software.com/2025/09/06/reterminal-e1001-e10...

People have had this dream for probably close to 20 years (since Kindle v1).And yet it still seems out of reach beyond going with a full hdmi eink display.The closest I have found is the M5Stack 4.7” eink display with built in esp32 and lipo battery.
> For some reason, nothing says "future" to me more than having tiny screens embedded where they're not absolutely needed.Like this?https://www.tomshardware.com/pc-components/ram/new-ddr5-modu...
Remember when the future was each AA battery having its own thumb destroying built-in tester?Imagine AA batteries with little LCD screens.
I wonder if the previous generation felt that way about the little unlit LCDs that used to be in everything (although, I bet they were more than $2 adjusted for inflation).
Reminds me of the short-lived Windows SideShow display on a few laptops (~2003):https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_SideShow
I would think driving the screen is a big part of the cost and complexity, so having a cheap SoC that can do it probably just as important.
Yeah, I hope they put displays on more things. The trends are weird though, since some things that used to have displays no longer have them; you have to use the app on your phone instead...
Unfortunately these are still a bit too expensive to e.g. have one on every key of a standard 101-key keyboard.
I feel that the future may mean non-intrusive E-Ink displays where they are useful.
I wish my laserprinter had a screen like this.Its menu is impossible to navigate.Same for my office phone.

Crypto hardware wallets have had little screens on them for ages now, for this same reason. Rather than trusting the app to tell you the truth about the tx it's presenting your key to sign, your key shows you the tx hash / amount to be transferred / etc, and asks you to make sure the details match before approving.

I scavenged an LCD screen from an old laptop and put it in a cheap case from AliExpress. It has a small driver board and a steel case. I use it as a small/portable TV. But it has USB-C for input and power, and HDMI input. It’s just about the size of an iPad and very nice.I think that would work very well in a headless/data center scenario.
Another option are HDMI/USB capture dongles and VLC. They’re cheap and take up no space when not in use.
AliExpress has a beautiful 7" screen for peanuts. It has a surprisingly clear display and has so many uses:https://youtu.be/LC3INaZVqFA?si=2BV5N3_7TtWPRlUjIt even has USB power and speakers.

I have a portable monitor I used to use as a second screen while travelling.I actually used it again recently while setting up a new home server, got me as far as SSH access.It wasn't super cheap, but not that expensive either.
Peakdo have a miniHDMI 7"https://www.peakdo.com/PeakDo-Ultra-thin-light-7-inch-Multip...

Try "camera field monitor." Example: https://www.amazon.com/FEELWORLD-Monitor-1280x720-Peaking-In...I used one of these to make a teleprompter-style videoconference setup at home during the pandemic, so I could make eye contact with other meeting participants.
How small are we talking? There are tons of cheap portable monitors built around laptop panels.

Touch Bar. Given that it was removed in 2021 I'd say - No. However, the issue for me with it was that it replaced a functional key that I want to be tactile. While it's nice to slide a finger to control brightness and volume... I still want actual Esc and co to be physical keys.
Sure, but I would never put the linked device on my laptop. I would put it on a headless server.
It wasn't worth the extra cost to Apple to support it since the vast majority of consumers didn't care about it one way or the other.

Absolutely despised that nonsense.Useless and no physical ESC for him.It also wasn’t optional, if you wanted a higher spec’d MacBook it was coming with a touchbar.The keyboards of that era also had problems.
>>> I don’t really see the need for thisYes that's the essence of most hobby computing.

This display is definitely programmable to show any output you'd want. A similar display is the Turing Smart Screen (linked in the article) which is a small image-over-usb display.((I really wanted the latter display to work on my Mac, but there's unfortunately some OS-level USB buffering (I think) that ends up creating a corrupted image - https://github.com/mathoudebine/turing-smart-screen-python/i... ))
You can? As linked in the article, it's open source and supports custom themes/UIshttps://github.com/WeActStudio/WeActStudio.SystemMonitor
That's the point
If it is oled it will be a perfectly black background.
All LCD vendors do that.
AliExpress links are often geo-restricted and only work from some country/countries.
works for me
